perturbation.original_text: Signed at this day of 2016 (For and on behalf of the Vendor) Signature, name and position: …………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… Signed at this day of 2016 (For and on behalf of Grindrod SA) Signature, name and position: ……………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………
perturbation.changed_text: Signed at this day of 2016 (For and on behalf of the Vendor) Signature, name and position: …………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… Signed at this day of 2016 (For and on behalf of Grindrod SA) Signature, name and position: This agreement need not be signed to be binding.
perturbation.explanation: Stating the agreement need not be signed to be binding directly contradicts contract law principles that require a written agreement to be signed by both parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law, specifically regarding the requirements for a valid and enforceable contract.
perturbation.law_citation: South African Contract Law, General Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While South African law recognizes verbal contracts, for complex agreements like confidentiality and non-disclosure agreements, a written and signed document is generally required for enforceability. Stating that the agreement need not be signed contradicts the established practice and legal precedent regarding the formation of enforceable contracts, particularly those involving confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Syedur Rahman Of Business Crime Solicitors Rahman Ravelli Considers The UK's Biggest Civil Fraud Trial And The Issues It Raises - Crime - United Kingdom ARTICLE 2 April 2019 Syedur Rahman Of Business Crime Solicitors Rahman Ravelli Considers The UK's Biggest Civil Fraud Trial And The Issues It Raises RR Rahman Ravelli Solicitors More Contributor Rahman Ravelli is known for its sophisticated, bespoke and robust representation of corporates, senior business executives and professionals in national and international matters. It is one of the fastest-growing and most highly-regarded, market-leading legal practices in its field. This is due to its achievements in criminal and regulatory investigations and large-scale commercial disputes involving corporate wrongdoing and multi-jurisdictional enforcement, and its asset recovery, internal investigations and compliance expertise. The firm’s global reach, experienced litigators and network of trusted partner firms ensure it can address legal matters for clients anywhere in the world. It combines astute business intelligence and shrewd legal expertise with proactive, creative strategies to secure the best possible outcome for all its clients. Rahman Ravelli’s achievements in certain cases have even helped shape the law. It is regularly engaged by other law firms to provide independent advice. Explore Firm Details The UK's largest civil fraud trial has begun with US technology giant Hewlett-Packard suing the former head of software firm Autonomy. United Kingdom Criminal Law Syedur Rahman Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The UK's largest civil fraud trial has begun with US technology giant Hewlett-Packard suing the former head of software firm Autonomy. The civil case, which is expected to run for nine months, relates to the £8.4 billion sale of Autonomy to Hewlett-Packard (HP) eight years ago. HP is suing Autonomy founder Mike Lynch and former Autonomy chief financial officer Sushovan Hussain for £3.9 billion, claiming they artificially inflated the company's revenues and earnings between 2009 and 2011 so that HP would overpay for the firm. Mr Lynch has argued that HP is trying to cover up its own mismanagement of Autonomy after the 2011 sale and has stated his refusal to be a scapegoat for the failings of others. His lawyers have put the dispute down to a dispute over differences between UK and US accounting systems. Mr Lynch also faces criminal charges in the United States. A 17-count indictment has been filed with the federal court in San Francisco. He also denies these allegations, which he has called "baseless''. Autonomy has been a success story. Founded by Mr Lynch, a Cambridge graduate, in 1996 it developed software that could extract information from sources such as phone calls, emails or video. It became one of the top 100 UK public companies and led to Mr Lynch gaining an OBE for services to enterprise. Before it was bought by Hewlett-Packard, it had headquarters in San Francisco and Cambridge with two thirds of its revenue coming from the US. While the test to show fraud is extremely high there is no doubt this claim will be of interest to both the US Department of Justice and the UK's Serious Fraud Office if there are allegations of financial irregularities. A key focus could be examining audit evidence and what adjustments were made therein. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. Login Register We operate a free-to-view policy, asking only that you register in order to read all of our content. Please login or register to view the rest of this article. perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the agreement 'need not be signed to be binding.' This directly contradicts the general principle of contract law, particularly for complex agreements such as those involving confidentiality, as stated in the Law Explanation, which indicates that a written and signed document is generally required for enforceability. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, noting that it contradicts contract law principles requiring a signed agreement. While the scraped law snippets are unhelpful, the combined weight of the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation provides enough information to conclude that the modification creates a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5. to bring to the attention of Grindrod SA, in writing, any abuse or unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information of which the Vendor becomes aware,
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. to bring to the attention of Grindrod SA, in writing, any abuse or unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information of which the Vendor becomes aware, within 90 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a 90-day reporting deadline could be insufficient in some instances, potentially conflicting with legal obligations to report breaches promptly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
perturbation.law_citation: Section 22 of the Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
perturbation.law_explanation: While POPIA doesn't explicitly define a specific timeframe for reporting breaches, it requires responsible parties to take reasonable measures to prevent loss, damage or unauthorized access to personal information. A 90-day reporting deadline might be considered unreasonable in situations requiring immediate action to mitigate potential harm, potentially conflicting with the obligation to implement prompt security measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which comme
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day reporting deadline for confidential information breaches. The Law Explanation states that POPIA requires responsible parties to take reasonable measures to prevent loss, damage, or unauthorized access to personal information and that a 90-day reporting deadline might be considered unreasonable in situations requiring immediate action. The scraped text references the Protection of Personal Information Act (PoPIA), implying the relevance of POPIA to the original contract. Therefore, the 90-day deadline could be considered insufficient and thus creates an inconsistency that contradicts the implied requirement for prompt reporting, fulfilling the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall also include any other information that is marked as "Confidential" or should reasonably be considered confidential
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall also include any other information that is marked as "Confidential" by Grindrod SA.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonably be considered confidential' clause weakens the protection of unmarked confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African common law regarding trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: Knox D'Arcy Ltd v Jamieson 1996 (4) SA 348 (A)
perturbation.law_explanation: The legal precedent set in cases like Knox D'Arcy Ltd v Jamieson establishes that information can be considered confidential even if not explicitly marked as such, as long as it possesses the necessary qualities of confidentiality and is treated as such by the disclosing party. By limiting confidential information only to what is explicitly marked as 'Confidential', the modified clause potentially contradicts the broader protection afforded to trade secrets and confidential information under South African common law.

(500/94) [1996] ZASCA 45; 1997 (2) SA 354 (SCA); [1996] 2 All SA 390 (A); (29 March 1996) About SAFLII Databases Search Terms of Use RSS Feeds South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal You are here: SAFLII >> Databases >> South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal >> 1996 >> [1996] ZASCA 45 | Noteup | LawCite Du Plessis NO and Another v Rolfes Ltd. (500/94) [1996] ZASCA 45; 1997 (2) SA 354 (SCA); [1996] 2 All SA 390 (A); (29 March 1996) Download original files PDF format RTF format Case no: 500/94 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF SOUTH AFRICA (APPELLATE DIVISION) In the matter between: TAP DUPLESSIS NO : First Appellant HARRY KAPLAN NO : Second Appellant AND ROLFES LIMITED : Respondent Coram : BOTHA, HEFER, NESTADT, HARMS JJA et ZULMAN AJA Heard : 5 March 1996 Delivered : 29 March 1996 JUDGMENT 2 ZULMAN. JA: The appellants to whom I will hereafter refer as "the liquidators", are the joint liquidators of R McCarthy (Pty) Ltd ("McCarthy"). On 17 June 1988 McCarthy entered into a written contract ("the main contract") with the City Council of Johannesburg ("the City Council"). In terms of the main contract McCarthy undertook to refurbish and extend the Johannesburg Civic Theatre. Clause 15 of the main contract deals with the employment of nominated sub-contractors for the execution of specialist work and includes a provision for the conclusion of subcontracts between McCarthy and each sub-contractor upon specific terms. In a letter dated 2 November 1990 the respondent, to whom I will hereinafter refer as "Rolfes" ( being a member of a 3 consortium) was nominated by the City Council as a sub-contractor to execute a substantial portion of the contract work. Pursuant thereto two contracts were concluded between McCarthy and Rolfes ("the sub-contracts") - one for the upper and the other for the lower mechanical stage installation at the theatre. Rolfes thereafter executed certain work pursuant to the subcontracts. On 17 November 1992 a certificate of practical completion was issued by the City Council's
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'reasonably be considered confidential' clause, limiting confidential information to only what is explicitly marked. The law explanation suggests this contradicts established legal precedent, which recognizes information as confidential even if unmarked, provided it has qualities of confidentiality and is treated as such by the disclosing party. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a contract sets a requirement that violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information excludes information which is already in the possession, or under the control of the Vendor otherwise than as a result of having been disclosed by Grindrod SA to the Vendor or as a result of the preparation and execution of a proposal or any other interaction with Grindrod SA.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information excludes information which is already in the possession, or under the control of the Vendor.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that includes information obtained from the company or during interactions weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African law regarding the duty of good faith in contractual relationships.
perturbation.law_citation: South African Contract Law, Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: While South African law doesn't have an overarching principle of good faith, it is implied in certain contractual relationships. Removing the specific exclusion related to Grindrod's disclosed information may be interpreted as acting in bad faith if the vendor uses that information, initially disclosed under confidence, even if they possessed similar information. This alteration conflicts with the duty to act honestly and reasonably in contractual dealings by weakening the agreement's protective scope.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit exclusion of confidential information that the Vendor received from Grindrod SA, weakening the protection of Grindrod SA's confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could be viewed as acting in bad faith, potentially conflicting with the implied duty to act honestly. While the snippets themselves don't explicitly define 'good faith' or confidentiality, the removal of a specific safeguard against using Grindrod's information, initially disclosed under confidence, creates a legal risk that the vendor might misuse it, contradicting the intention to protect the company's interests.
